name: IPCC Overview
description:
A straight cause-and-effect story of global warming.

The main culprits — power & heat, transport, buildings, heavy industry, oil & gas operations, and land-use change — drive CO₂ (and some methane). Rising CO₂ strengthens the greenhouse effect and acidifies the ocean → warming, extremes, marine stress → food and people.

Feedback: warming thaws permafrost → more methane → stronger greenhouse effect.

Click any card for detail and IPCC sources.
credit: Pedagogical idea inspired by La Fresque du Climat / Climate Fresk (Cédric Ringenbach). This simplified spine and wording are original, based on IPCC AR6 — not a copy of the official deck.
disclaimer: Not an official Climate Fresk product. Official materials are CC BY-NC-ND. Educational summary only — see cited IPCC chapters for authoritative text.

cards.label: Land-use change
cards.summary: Deforestation and land conversion — the non-fossil CO₂ culprit
cards.details: Alongside the fossil system, clearing forests and draining peat releases carbon stored in ecosystems. It is not “fossil fuel”, but it is a major human CO₂ source and weakens natural sinks.
cards.key_figures: AFOLU (agriculture, forestry and land use) is a major emissions category and also a potential sink.

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 5 — Global Carbon Cycle
IPCC AR6 WG3 Summary for Policymakers

cards.label: CO₂ emissions
cards.summary: Extra carbon dioxide poured into the air each year
cards.details: These activities add CO₂ faster than natural sinks can remove it. What matters for long-term warming is the cumulative total.
cards.key_figures: Cumulative CO₂ largely sets long-term warming (Transient Climate Response to Emissions).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG1 Summary for Policymakers
IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 5 — Global Carbon Cycle

cards.label: More CO₂ in the air
cards.details: About half of emitted CO₂ stays in the atmosphere for decades to centuries; the rest is taken up by land and ocean — for now.
cards.key_figures: CO₂ is higher than at any time in at least 2 million years (AR6).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG1 Summary for Policymakers
IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 5 — Global Carbon Cycle

cards.label: Stronger greenhouse effect
cards.summary: Extra CO₂ traps more outgoing heat
cards.details: The natural greenhouse effect makes Earth habitable. Human-added CO₂ strengthens it, so the planet must warm until energy balance is restored.
cards.key_figures: Well-mixed GHGs provide the largest positive radiative forcing since 1750 (WG1 Ch7).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 7 — Energy budget
IPCC AR6 WG1 Summary for Policymakers

cards.label: Ocean takes up CO₂
cards.summary: Seawater dissolves a large share of our CO₂
cards.details: The ocean slows the rise of atmospheric CO₂ — but the dissolved CO₂ changes seawater chemistry.
cards.key_figures: The ocean has absorbed a substantial fraction of anthropogenic CO₂ (WG1 Ch5).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 5 — Global Carbon Cycle
IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 9 — Ocean & cryosphere

cards.label: Global warming
cards.summary: The planet’s surface and oceans heat up
cards.details: Most excess heat goes into the ocean. Air temperatures rise, ice melts, and weather patterns shift.
cards.key_figures: ~1.1°C warmer in 2011–2020 vs 1850–1900; warming is unequivocally human-driven (AR6 SPM).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG1 Summary for Policymakers
IPCC AR6 Synthesis Report

cards.label: Ocean acidification
cards.summary: More CO₂ makes seawater more acidic
cards.details: Dissolved CO₂ forms carbonic acid. pH falls and carbonate ions drop — harder for shells and reefs to build.
cards.key_figures: Ocean acidification is a direct chemical consequence of CO₂ uptake (WG1 Ch5 / Ch9).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 5 — Global Carbon Cycle
IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 9 — Ocean & cryosphere

cards.label: Worse extremes
cards.details: A warmer atmosphere holds more moisture and loads more energy into extremes. Attribution links many changes to human warming.
cards.key_figures: Hot extremes have become more frequent and intense (WG1 Ch11).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 11 — Extremes
IPCC AR6 WG1 Summary for Policymakers

cards.label: Marine life under stress
cards.summary: Coral reefs, shellfish and food webs suffer
cards.details: Acidification plus warming (marine heatwaves) bleach corals, stress calcifying organisms, and rearrange ocean ecosystems.
cards.key_figures: Warm-water corals are among the systems at highest risk (WG2).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG2 Summary for Policymakers
IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 9 — Ocean & cryosphere

cards.label: Food & fisheries
cards.summary: Crops and seafood become less reliable
cards.details: Heat, drought, floods and collapsing reefs/fisheries hit harvests and protein from the sea — hardest on the most exposed communities.
cards.key_figures: Climate change has already affected food security in several regions (WG2 SPM).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG2 Summary for Policymakers

cards.label: People & health
cards.details: Extremes and food stress cascade into health risks, unsafe work, migration pressure and economic loss — shaped by inequality and governance.
cards.key_figures: Health and livelihood risks rise with every increment of warming (WG2 / SYR).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG2 Summary for Policymakers
IPCC AR6 Synthesis Report

cards.label: Cut emissions
cards.summary: Stop adding CO₂ at the source
cards.details: Clean power, efficiency, protecting forests and cutting methane are how we slow the whole chain. Sinks help but cannot replace deep fossil CO₂ cuts.
cards.key_figures: Limiting warming requires global GHG emissions to peak ASAP and fall sharply this decade (SYR / WG3).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG3 Summary for Policymakers
IPCC AR6 Synthesis Report

cards.label: Permafrost thaw
cards.summary: Frozen ground thaws as the Arctic warms
cards.details: Permafrost stores vast amounts of organic carbon locked in frozen soils. As air and ground temperatures rise, previously frozen ground thaws, exposing that carbon to microbes.
cards.key_figures: Large permafrost carbon stocks are vulnerable to thaw under continued warming (IPCC AR6 WG1).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 5 — Global Carbon Cycle
IPCC AR6 WG1 Summary for Policymakers

cards.label: Methane release
cards.summary: Thawing soils release CH₄ (and CO₂) — a climate feedback
cards.details: Microbes break down thawed organic matter and can produce methane (CH₄), a potent greenhouse gas. That extra warming agent is not from burning fossil fuels — it is the Earth system amplifying human-caused warming. This closes a vicious cycle: more warming → more thaw → more methane → stronger greenhouse effect → more warming.
cards.key_figures: Methane is shorter-lived than CO₂ but much stronger per molecule over decades; permafrost carbon feedbacks increase projected warming (AR6).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 5 — Global Carbon Cycle
IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 6 — Short-lived climate forcers

cards.label: Power & heat
cards.summary: Coal and gas plants for electricity and district heat
cards.details: Electricity and heat generation is the single largest fossil CO₂ slice in many inventories: coal and gas burned in power stations and heat plants. Decarbonizing the grid cuts emissions across buildings, industry and transport as they electrify.
cards.key_figures: Energy supply (electricity & heat) is typically the largest sectoral CO₂ source globally (IPCC AR6 WG3).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG3 Summary for Policymakers
IPCC AR6 Synthesis Report

cards.label: Transport
cards.summary: Cars, trucks, ships and planes — mostly oil
cards.details: Road vehicles dominate transport CO₂; aviation and shipping add fast-growing oil demand. Almost all of this still comes from liquid fossil fuels.
cards.key_figures: Transport is among the largest end-use CO₂ sectors and is highly oil-dependent (WG3).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG3 Summary for Policymakers

cards.label: Buildings
cards.summary: Heating, cooling and cooking in homes and workplaces
cards.details: Buildings burn gas and oil on-site and pull fossil electricity from the grid. Insulation, heat pumps and a clean grid cut both direct and indirect emissions.
cards.key_figures: Buildings account for a large share of final energy use; emissions depend on the fuel mix (WG3).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG3 Summary for Policymakers

cards.label: Heavy industry
cards.summary: Steel, cement, chemicals — fuel + process CO₂
cards.details: Blast furnaces, cement kilns and chemical plants burn fossil fuels and also release process CO₂ (e.g. limestone → lime in cement). Harder to electrify than cars or home heat.
cards.key_figures: Industry is one of the largest emitting sectors; cement and steel are standout process emitters (WG3).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG3 Summary for Policymakers

cards.label: Oil & gas ops
cards.summary: Extraction, flaring, vents and methane leaks
cards.details: Before fuel is burned by end users, the oil and gas system itself emits: flaring, venting, and methane leaks along wells, pipes and processing. That is both CO₂ and CH₄ from the supply chain.
cards.key_figures: Fossil fuel production and distribution are major CH₄ sources as well as CO₂ (WG1 Ch6 / WG3).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 6 — Short-lived climate forcers
IPCC AR6 WG3 Summary for Policymakers
